It's the sims. The base game can be fun, and can be boring, depending on what you choose to do or the traits you give your sims. Now the extension packs. A few opinions, take them or leave them. I would recommend Sims 4 (plus it's free so why nah), but only recommend one or two extension packs just because of the price.

Great game, loved the Sims since the first one, but this is a great game even in its base form without any add-ons-- you can install mods to add in some functionality and extra items if you want with no additional packs. I recommend a few packs though. I like Vampires for the occult, I like Island Living for the world (Sulani), and Eco Lifestyle for the green-living stuff and dumpster diving! If you like farming, get Eco Lifestyle, Cottage Living, and Horse Ranch as they all go really well together. Seasons is okay if you want to have holidays and the titular seasons, but it adds in seasonality to all crops and makes any electronics outside (not under a roof) break in snow or rain. I'm not a huge fan of the pet system in the game, but if you want to try it out, that's Cats and Dogs.  For smaller game packs, Tiny Living is a big help! Most of the stuff packs are pretty good, depending on what you're looking for and if you like building and designing houses (or Sims!)  Aliens are in Get to Work which are super fun and your Sims can get abducted and even get pregnant by the aliens (regardless of gender/sex).   Lovestruck has a great world with great characters/townies, but the actual gameplay features makes it so romance becomes a full-time job, but does add a lot of agency to the other Sims.   City Living adds in food stands-- everywhere! Very, very useful feature to have, and there's also festivals and new recipes to learn (and apartments!) It also adds in some fun new career paths.  Get Famous and I have a love-hate relationship. I love the added celebrity status (great for challenge runs) but the already-established celebs showing up really throws off a lot of things when the fans show up as well with paparazzi, and can disturb everything from events to dates and a lot more than that.   Parenthood and Growing Together... not my favourites, to be sure. I like the self-discovery feature that lets Sims have up to six traits, but it adds a lot of really annoying gameplay into infants and they glitch out a lot, even now with supposed fixes. If I have those packs installed, I usually age up the infant after a day or so to just completely avoid the headaches. Or immediately, depending on my fancy.  Overall, great game. It works fine on my Steamdeck (set up at a desk with a mouse, keyboard, and monitor as a full desktop). It does occasionally fully break everything, and refuses to load, to save, or do anything, so I recommend frequent save backups if you are also playing on Steamdeck. Learn from my endless pain. I've had to completely uninstall and reinstall the game twice now. Currently playing base game only (not even any stuff packs installed) and it's working great even with a couple mods!

The Sims 4  is one of the most iconic life simulation franchises, offering players complete freedom to create, control, and shape virtual lives. From building detailed houses to managing relationships, careers, and daily routines, the game provides endless creative possibilities.  Its open-ended gameplay allows players to tell their own stories without strict goals, making every experience unique. Expansion packs add even more depth, introducing new worlds, activities, and customization options. However, the base game can sometimes feel limited without additional content, and certain systems may appear repetitive over long play sessions.  Overall, The Sims remains a highly creative and relaxing simulation experience that continues to define the life-sim genre.

since the early 2010s, i’ve been part of this scene. it started with playing the sims freeplay up to level 30 on my dad's samsung tab 2. i was obsessed with the time-consuming limited-time event rewards and packing my houses full of them. when i found out a full pc version existed, i spent ages looking for a way to play it for free, but had no luck and stuck to freeplay. eventually, as a kid with no pocket money, i downloaded a pirated copy just to taste what it was like.   this game honestly helped shape who i am. it made me swallow my pride, bring my head down from the clouds, and finally come out of my shell. as someone who deals with severe anxiety, it pushed me to socialise and express myself in ways i couldn't in the real world. it also completely birthed my deep investment into the simulation genre and its history.   growing up, i didn't actually play with physical dollhouses, but i'd see my friends with them or spot them in tv ads and absolutely drool over wanting to build one. for years as a little kid, i would play with building blocks shaped like houses and build house fortresses out of cushions. i constantly dreamt of playing pretend with a house. i even bunked a class in grade 5 just to roam the school grounds under a palm tree, improvising a home in my head. this game just ticked all my boxes. it became the ultimate digital sandbox of that childhood dream, letting me build, design, and control everything.   the soundtrack was absolutely iconic and kept me hooked for hours. on top of that, modding is incredibly seamless. i even made a small mod myself back in 2021 and was amazed by how accessible the tools were.  the hours you see on my steam profile (around 11 hours at the time of writing) don't show the real picture. i've been playing this game day in, day out since 2017, letting my imagination run completely wild.   the only reason this game went from holding a "special place" in my heart to holding a "special place in the past" is corporate greed. when a game requires nearly $1,000 usd in dlcs just to feel complete, it completely kills the enthusiasm. ea literally milked the player base dry before finally making the base game free-to-play. while i've ditched piracy now that i can support games properly, companies that corporate-greed their products this hard almost invite it. the constant churning out of low-effort dlcs has caused my enthusiasm to decline, but i still hold onto a bit of hope that they can turn things around and bring back the magic this franchise used to have.

I have been a Simmer since the 4th grade, when I started with The Sims 2. I wasn't a big fan of The Sims 4 at first, at it was released with bare bones. The original game was supposed to be multiplayer, but that aspect of the game was scrapped last minute, resulting in a lackluster game at launch. It has been over a decade now since the launch of The Sims 4 and it has improved substantially. However, not enough. Compared to previous games in the series, The Sims 4 just falls short. I think part of this is due to the rough start the game had and partly due to the greed of EA. If you were to buy all of the DLC for this game, you would be spending over a grand. I wouldn't mind this if the DLC was worth the price. In previous Sims games, you got more value for your buck. Now, you only get about half of what you used to get and sometimes its just recolors or retextured. I will forever love this game (I also have a Sims related tattoo), but I don't think my loyalty will be forever, especially now that Paralives has released and has proved it can do just as well, or even better, than EA at including content and features in early access.   There is also a troubling part to this story that has developed recently: The EA Buyout. While it hasn't gone through yet, it is still worrisome. If this deal goes through, it will be a deal breaker for me and I will no longer support The Sims. It's hard for me to give up a game I have loved for most of my life...but the franchise will no longer align with my beliefs if this buyout happens. I will fight for The Sims until then and I hope that in the future, EA will care about their customers and fan base.

This is genuinely one of the most disappointing games I've ever played.  Every few months it's, "Hey! Here's a new $35 content pack!" And what does it include? One outfit, a slightly different-colored bed, and a handful of items that probably should've been in a previous pack you already paid for.  I've been playing The Sims since Sims 2, and for years I just accepted EA's practices because there wasn't really another life simulation game offering the same experience. But that monopoly made the developers complacent. They knew people would keep buying packs because there weren't any real alternatives.  Now there are alternatives. While I'm personally not a fan of some of Inzoi's AI usage, and Paralives is still in early access, both games have already demonstrated a level of passion and innovation that The Sims has been lacking for years.  The Sims community has spent ages asking for features that would genuinely improve gameplay—things like an open world, better customization tools, color sliders for clothing and furniture, and deeper gameplay systems. Instead, we keep getting overpriced packs filled with recycled assets and shallow content.  Even when the developers do listen, it often feels like the bare minimum. Players begged for eyelashes for years, and when we finally got them, they're so limited that most people immediately go back to using custom content anyway.  And can someone explain why I need to enter cheats just to freely place a candle on a table in a game that's supposed to be about creativity? Why are basic building functions locked behind console commands? I'll be uninstalling the game and I have no intention of coming back. I'd honestly recommend taking a look at Paralives. Not sponsored, just a genuinely refreshing reminder that developers can still care about their players.  The Sims used to feel like a passion project. Now it feels like a storefront with a game attached to it.

I have a lot of hours in The Sims 4 and genuinely enjoyed my time with it. The freedom it gives players is amazing, whether you're building houses, creating characters, or telling stories. It's very easy to lose track of time while playing.  Unfortunately, I can't recommend it.  My biggest issue is that after more than 25 years of The Sims as a franchise, there is still no official Turkish language support. Considering EA is one of the biggest gaming companies in the world and the Turkish gaming community is far from small, that's hard to justify.  The pricing makes it even worse. Players in Türkiye are expected to pay premium prices for countless expansions and DLC while receiving none of the basic localization support many other regions get.  I do appreciate the recent bug fixes, quality-of-life improvements, and events. However, despite these updates, I don't really feel any desire to return to the game. It often feels like these changes only started happening once The Sims finally had some competition in the life simulation genre.

The Sims will always be a first choice for a lot of people in the life simulation genre. It's easy, fun, and is pretty much an architecture simulator and interior design simulator as much as it is a "life" simulator. In fact, arguable it does that better than the life aspect. I [b] genuinely [/b] tried to give this game a fair chance.  I grew up with The Sims 2 and The Sims 3, so I already knew about most of the criticism surrounding this game when it released back in 2014, but I still wanted to experience it for myself before judging it. After finally putting time into it years later, I understand exactly why so many longtime players feel disappointed.  The Sims 4 is probably the most aesthetically polished game in the franchise, and that is unfortunately where most of the praise ends for me.  There ARE aspects of the game that initially look appealing. The graphics are clean, the art style aged decently, and the Create-A-Sim system is accessible enough for casual players to immediately have fun with.  But the deeper you get into the game, the more restrictive everything starts to feel.  Even the build mode people constantly praise begins falling apart the second you want to create something beyond the game’s carefully limited systems. Suddenly you need cheats, debug menus, TOOL mod, Better BuildBuy, custom content, and twenty browser tabs open just to make the game feel flexible enough to properly express creativity.  And that becomes the core issue with The Sims 4 as a whole: so much of the experience depends on outside labor from the community instead of the actual game itself.  At some point you realize most of the best Sims 4 content is not even made by EA. The community fixes bugs EA ignores for years. Modders add more meaningful gameplay depth than official expansions. Custom content creators carry the game’s aesthetic variety almost entirely on their backs.  A life simulation game should not require this much external effort just to feel complete, immersive, or creatively satisfying.  Underneath the pretty graphics and smooth build mode is a life simulation game that feels incredibly shallow compared to its predecessors. The Sims 2 had insane attention to detail, personality, charm, and little interactions that made sims feel alive. The Sims 3 had an open world, story progression, unpredictability, and actual depth to gameplay. Sims had individuality. Households around town evolved on their own. Things felt chaotic in a fun way.  The Sims 4 somehow feels emptier than games that came out over a decade before it.  The emotion system that was marketed as revolutionary quickly becomes repetitive and surface-level. Traits barely matter because most sims react the same way to everything. Conversations repeat endlessly. Gameplay loops become stale extremely fast. The game feels less like a life simulator and more like a virtual dollhouse constantly interrupted by loading screens.  And then there’s the monetization.  Calling this game “free to play” is honestly laughable when the actual full experience costs an absurd amount of money. Hundreds upon hundreds of dollars worth of DLC, expansion packs, kits, stuff packs, and micro-content — and somehow the game STILL feels incomplete. Features that absolutely should have been base-game content are sold back to players piece by piece.  What makes it worse is that EA keeps pumping out new packs while bugs that have existed for literal years remain unfixed. Sims stop responding. Saves corrupt. Events break. Careers glitch. Pathfinding randomly implodes. Sometimes entire saves become unusable even without mods installed.  And yet somehow there is always another $40 expansion ready to release.  At some point the community itself became responsible for keeping this game playable. Players rely heavily on mods not only to improve gameplay, but sometimes to outright fix issues EA never addresses. Mod creators have genuinely added more meaningful depth to this game than many official packs have.  But even that becomes exhausting.  Once you start looking for mods or custom content, you suddenly have to dig through dozens of sketchy websites, ad-ridden pages, broken links, subscription-gated downloads, early-access paywalls, Discord servers, and random Patreon pages just to make the game feel complete or personalized. Want a different aesthetic? Better gameplay? More realistic interactions? Functional systems? Prepare to spend hours mod-hunting and risk downloading malware because the vanilla experience barely offers enough depth to stay engaging on its own.  And despite all this, the game constantly throws advertisements and promotional popups at you trying to sell even more content. Nothing kills immersion faster than opening your supposedly relaxing life simulation game and immediately getting blasted with ads for another overpriced DLC.  The saddest part is that The Sims franchise used to feel innovative. It used to feel creative. It used to feel like a genuine passion project centered around storytelling and simulation. The Sims 4 feels corporate. It feels engineered around monetization first and gameplay second.

First red flag for this game was having to download the EA app to even play it. Then, after booting it up, got four different pop-ups before even getting to press a single button on the main menu.   Wanted to overall play this game because I enjoy building and decorating, and wanted to do so in a certain way involving mods. However, a ton of the mods to do so are locked behind paywalls or on websites that requires subscriptions, payments, and sign-ups to even view said mods. Which, ultimately kills the whole vibe and desire to play this game in the first place. Paying around $10+ per mod/custom creation just to give the game a early 2000's grudge theme -- or any theme other than base-game is awful, you shouldn't have to make an account or pay money or to even risk downloading malware just for something so simple. It's game changing, but not THAT game changing, people.  Worst part is; I've played this game mod-less in the past. It can be interesting for some time, but loses interest very quickly. Lots of content behind paywalls for in-game currency, not a lot to work with, tons of pop ups, and anything you can use is boring or repetitive. Which puts you in a position: buy stuff with real money (which there's a lot of stuff with too high of a price tag on each) or deal with what you got (which isn't anything impressive).  Very disappointing, only played the game for four minutes and researched everything for ten minutes. So, fourteen minutes in total, and I'm already pre-planned writing this review so when I hit the 'time in-game' limit to write a review, I can just post this in and not have to worry about this game again. I don't like to immediately judge a game off the bat, but I knew everything about this game when it came out in 2014, and it failed horribly when I gave it a chance twelve years later. 4/10 for the possibility to play it, and a 0/10 for the paywalls and pop-ups.

Now, I have almost 600 hours in the Sims on Steam. This doesn’t include my EA time or the hours I spent playing The Sims 3 throughout my life. Although the game is great with all the packs and kits, it’s only a building block of a game without them, unless you have mods installed. The game has been out for OVER A DECADE, so the base game should definitely be much more than just a building block. The DLC is so expensive, it's basically just a cash-grab simulator at this point. And every time they update to fix one problem, another problem surfaces, breaking both the base game and/or the mods people have installed. I will restate this again: the base game is basically empty. Unless you start pouring money right into EA’s pockets—which can be thousands—you’re basically getting a demo, and that’s it. The EA team makes half-assed DLCs. The first DLC that I thought had so much untouched potential was the Magic Realms expansion. The world was super small with only 5 lots, and the buildings looked like they were done, not by a professional, but by a first-time player with zero building experience. The townies that came with the pack, as well, are very lacking, unlike the townies that came with Get Famous or City Living, and I feel like they could have expanded on the witchy gameplay, the build mode items, and the CAS items. However, the packs that I think are really good are Seasons and City Living, but I feel like they should be a part of the base game. I also feel that some of the gameplay in the Growing Together and parenthood pack has things that should be in the base game as well. So even if you decide to rain money on them, you just get a few things that, in my opinion, should be in the base game and shouldn't cost $140. I honestly think the best way EA can fix their rep and their game is to revamp the graphics and update some of the older items to look newer. They should take some of the most well-known mods and ask permission to use the idea; they could also add the creator to the EA team. For instance, The Witcher 3 added community content to the base game with a next-gen update. EA isn’t doing that. They prefer to earn more money with their marketplace scam, which does incorporate community content, but it's for a price and has broken trust in a lot of their players. Oh, and they need to stop treating their customers like a wallet to use instead of a customer to please. I could sit here and write things I think the base game needs for hours on end, which is just the problem, but I’m going to leave this review—which is basically just a rant—right here.

I've been playing The Sims since Sims 2, and it genuinely hurts to write this review.    The Sims 4 isn't unplayable because it's a bad game at its core …  it's unplayable because EA refuses to respect the people who actually built this community. Every major update is a gamble. Will your mods break? Will a base game mechanic that worked fine suddenly be bugged? Will EA "fix" something nobody asked them to touch? The answer is almost always yes.    The update cycle is exhausting. Longtime players have learned to dread patch days more than broken saves. And instead of addressing years of complaints from fans who have been here since the beginning, the game keeps pivoting toward a younger, trendier audience … flooding the store with packs that feel like sponsored content rather than meaningful gameplay additions.    Those of us who fell in love with the depth and creativity of Sims 2 have been patient for a long time. That patience is running out.    If you're new to The Sims, you might enjoy it at face value. If you're a veteran hoping this is the sequel the franchise deserved … it's not. Maybe not ever.

It's nowhere near as good as Sims 2 was back in the day, especially if you like to build homes in the game.    On the plus side, EA lets you play the base game for free, as long as you agree to four separate sets of terms and conditions, including a EULA, an acceptance of DRM, a promise to give them one of your children and maybe your dog, and an agreement that they can place MI6-level tracking devices on your PC forever. Oh, and of course you also have to create an account with them if you don't already have one.  But you know, free base game, so don't complain, right?   Sorry, Fido, we'll miss you.   Only the ugliest of furnishings is included in base game, but hey, there's about sixteen hundred dollars worth of DLC that may or may not be better.  And a 'marketplace' is built into the game screen so you can't even play the game without being reminded that they want you to spend money.    Do I fault them for wanting to be profitable? Of course not. I spent hundreds of dollars on Sims 2 getting all of the packs and stuff years ago. But that was when we could easily download free mods that would make the game better in various ways. It's not so easy to do that with Sims 4, although it would be if they'd just allow a Steam workshop. Which they won't.  It's too bad, because I'd happily buy DLC like the old packs and stuff if I could also supplement the game with a few decent user-created mods. Instead, I'm not going to buy any DLC.    Do I recommend the game? Not really. The whole base game feels like it exists just to hook you into impulse-purchasing DLC. But if you want to check it out for yourself, just sacrifice 26 gig of PC, agree to a bunch of nebulous stuff that probably makes it illegal to even read this review, let alone write it, and see for yourself.  Edit: Less than 24 hours after I posted this review, and only 48 hours after downloading the game and signing up for an EA account through Steam,  I was told by EA that they were banning my account. Since I've done nothing to violate their rules (I've literally only built a couple of houses in game and then not even saved when closing the game), I can only guess that the reason they're banning me is due to my review of their product.  If my choices are to take down this review so I can play the game or leave up this review and not play the game anymore, I'm choosing to leave up this review.

I’ve been playing The Sims 4 since 2016. So let me tell you this: this game started as a legend, but slowly turned into a pile of scams and disappointment. I never thought Electronic Arts would become this corporate mess where, instead of fixing their games, they just add more bugs through “DLCs.” The DLCs barely add any actual gameplay — just a couple of clothes, hairstyles, and mechanics that somehow break your game even more.  It’s been 4 years since I last played The Sims 4, and after this little marathon, I felt like I had to write this review. Honestly, I don’t think I’ll ever go back to being the loyal fan I once was. I only downloaded the game again to reevaluate it one last time before writing this review (for context, I used to play through Origin for hours every single day until 4 years ago), and the experience just reminded me why I stopped in the first place.  Cancelling The Sims 5 project — or “Project Rene,” as they used to call it — already made no sense. It honestly feels like they realized they could keep putting minimal effort into The Sims 4, adding even more bugs, and continuing to squeeze money out of players instead. What’s even crazier is that Electronic Arts still refuses to properly fix The Sims 4 or the countless broken packs that cost some players a whole kidney.  At this point, it feels less like a life simulation game and more like a never-ending cash grab held together by mods and player patience. EA once ruled the life simulation category, but now even their own fandom is sick of them. People are making and supporting new simulation games just to ruin the power EA once had over the genre.  The game is almost unplayable: optimization problems, bugs, extremely limited mechanics, and empty animations that barely improved or added anything new over time — you should see the animation details they put into Sims 3; those developers genuinely loved their game and players. I’m saying this as someone who was genuinely fascinated by the franchise's adaption to hate culture. Seeing how EA keeps adding more shame to its name over the years — alongside Riot, but hey, at least EA isn’t alone. The Sims 3 is honestly much better than 4 when it comes to mechanics and animations — and it scams less with DLCs too.  And like EA said, it’s been a decade already, so what’s the point of still having DLCs that now cost thousands of dollars in total? Maybe they could make some DLCs free, or at least include them in EA Play.  Hope Electronic Arts gets a grip one day. Till then, I’ll stick with games that actually promise future content without charging extra for every tiny thing: inZOI and Paralives.  Long story short, not recommended.

I used to play this game all the time, but I just cannot bring myself to play it much ever since the buyout was announced.    For those of you who don't know, EA is getting bought by Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund (PIF) and Jared Kushner’s Affinity Partners.  PIF is owned by THE Saudi Arabian Government, which is known for committing human rights abuses.  The game WAS fun but I will never buy an EA product again with these new owners profiting from it, for I am not comfortable with any of my purchases supporting such heinous crimes!  I urge YOU to follow suit!     EDIT: After reading some very valid comments I would like to address a few concerns surrounding my playtime and EA already being flawed before the buy-out.  None of these comments are wrong accept for the virtue signaling is a misconception.  My hours playing the game HAS increased despite my mentioning that I have stopped playing.  I would like to clarify that I launched the game to take pictures of my characters and builds that are important to me for documentation purposes only.    As an artist and aspiring author, I used to pay sims as a tool to make houses and make my characters I am using for my story so I can get the details right and so on.  I was using the game to transfer my characters to other games that I have transitioned to using to store all my characters.  These games I am now using are Tomodachi Life and hopefully Paralives when I can afford it, for those who are curious for alternatives.  Also I am aware the game has many issues besides the one I decided to highlight in my review earlier.  Going over all the issues would be a novel, and I personally don't think I would explain those well.    My goal of the review was not to point out every flaw, you can find much better reviews going over those issues elsewhere.  My goal of this review was to instead point out the recent buy-out and bring awareness to people interested in this game who might not research much before buying.

I would give this game a 5.8/10 overall, with a conditional recommendation depending on how it’s played.    👍 What I Recommend    I do recommend this game if you’re using the right mods and playing with clear boundaries. With the right setup, it can be great for:    Long-term save files across multiple generations  Storytelling and roleplay  YouTube content creation  Streaming    The modding community is a huge part of what makes the game enjoyable and customizable.    ⚠️ Major Concerns    However, there are some serious drawbacks:    High PC requirements: You need a strong computer to run the game smoothly, especially with mods installed.  Unregulated mods: Some mods can include inappropriate content such as:  Not-safe-for-work material  Sexual content  Smoking, vaping, and tobacco-related content  Because of this, it’s important to be careful about what you download and who has access to the game.  👤 Age Recommendation    I would recommend this game for ages 16 and up, mainly due to mod content and online access risks. Anything under 16 should stick to the vanilla (unmodded) version.    💰 DLC Opinion    The DLC situation is also mixed. There are a lot of expansions, but:    They can become very expensive (potentially over $200 total)  Many feel unnecessary unless you are heavily invested in the game    Personally, I wouldn’t recommend buying all DLC unless you’re fully committed to spending a lot of time in the game.    ⭐ Final Verdict  Score: 5.8/10  Best for: Story-driven players, content creators, and mod users  Not ideal for: Younger players or those expecting a simple, plug-and-play experience

I’ve been playing The Sims my whole life, started with 1, spent most of my time on Sims 2, and played a bit of 3 as well. With Sims 4, I went all in: bought every expansion, every kit, put in thousands of hours, and tried pretty much every mod out there.    At this point, I’m honestly done. It just gets boring. No mod has been able to bring back the kind of depth and personality that Sims 2 had, and it feels like nothing can really fix that.    The only thing that might help is something like the ACR mod from Sims 2—something that adds meaningful attraction and autonomy without turning everything chaotic like WickedWhims does. But even then, there are so many other issues. If you don’t constantly push them, Sims barely interact—sometimes they won’t even greet each other on a date.    It just feels like a lifeless game, and so far, nothing has really been able to bring that sense of life back into it.

Let's break this down; 651 hours +(380 hours on steam) . That's over a full month of my life poured into this game, a staggering investment of time and emotional energy. And what do we get in return? A development team that's effectively thrown in the towel, handing off their creation to some faceless corporate entity like it's just another asset to liquidate.    Even worse, the game itself has become borderline unplayable. Despite countless troubleshooting attempts; driver updates, reinstalls, tweaking settings. It still stutters, crashes, and outright sabotages basic gameplay functions like household management. The instability isn't just frustrating; it feels like betrayal.    And then there's the AI integration. Instead of addressing core issues! the bugs, the performance, the abandonment , they're slapping on half-baked "innovations" nobody asked for. It reeks of desperation, a hollow attempt to justify neglect with buzzword-driven "features" that only highlight how far the game has strayed from what made it worthwhile in the first place.    This isn't disappointment. This is the exhaustion of watching something you loved get mismanaged into irrelevance.

The update that finally made me quit was when EA decided to push an update to 'Modernize' the create a sim menu (Causing just about every mod that I had related to that to break irreparably as most mod authors had long since stopped making content for the game) and then they also pushed an update that completely broke a mod that allowed you to place objects without restrictions (e.g. place them diagonally and whatnot).  You also cannot roll the game back as it requires for you to be online in order to get all your DLC, which is exacerbated if you had the game's DLC divided between Steam and Origin like I used to.  Overall I wouldn't recommend it unless you have a lot of patience, you need a lot of mods to replicate what the sims 2 and 3 do at a significantly lower price.

TLDR: I regret the time and money I spent on this game and cannot recommend that anyone get invested in it anymore. It will only go downhill from here.  I've tried for years to truly love Sims 4, but EA somehow always finds a way to lower the bar again and again. The Marketplace update is already a shameless predatory cash grab, but on top of that, they've left the game unplayable for many people despite saying that the broken update is "fixed." It's been over a month since I was even able to launch the game. Even if they did actually manage to fix what they've broken, I don't think I'm willing to play anymore due to their recent business decisions (Marketplace update monetizing mods & the Saudi Arabian PIF takeover of EA).  I stopped buying DLC a while ago (except Life & Death EP) because they feel so lifeless, broken, and incompatible for the price tag that EA is placing them at. Most assets and game play mechanics feel rushed, reused, or fail to meet promised expectations. If they weren't so expensive, I wouldn't be holding them to a higher standard, but these aren't even worth buying during the 50% off sales. You used to get much more for the same price or less with some of the older Expansion Packs, Game Packs, and Stuff Packs, but they don't even make the latter two anymore. All they care about now is making half-hearted EPs and kits, and now this new Marketplace scheme.  I won't say I didn't enjoy the time I spent playing The Sims 4. Many, many hours have been spent creating Sims, imagining storylines, and building lots that I enjoy in hindsight. But just thinking about playing the Sims is honestly more fun than actually playing it at this point. It will never meet your expectations and you'll have to fight against the game's mechanics and bugs to try to accomplish what you'd been hoping for. Mods used to be helpful for picking up EA's slack, but over time, they have been finding ways to monetize even community creations.  I will not be spending any more of my time and money on this broken cash grab and neither should you.

I would not recommend this game despite being a frequent player; if you have never played it and are thinking about installing it for the first time, do yourself a favor and don't.  It is impossible to ignore the elephant in the room, and I cannot recommend this game to anyone who values their time and money. I’m not saying it’s not fun—it is—but that fun comes more from what you put into it with your imagination and your own stories than from what the game actually offers you in a tangible way. It is a life simulator that feels lifeless if you aren't the one pushing every single interaction, and that is a fundamental design flaw that they have tried to patch with a massive mountain of DLC.  Those of us who come from previous entries remember with affection expansions like The Sims 3: Supernatural. In a single DLC, you had vampires, werewolves, fairies, witches, and even zombies. It was a complete package, a whole new universe. In The Sims 4, EA decided it was a better idea to sell us the same thing in pieces. Want vampires? Buy a DLC. Want werewolves? Buy another one. Spellcasters? Yet another one. In the end, to have the same experience we had 10 years ago, you have to spend triple the money. It’s as if they sold you a car but charged you for the steering wheel, the tires, and the mirrors separately. And that is just one example among many other DLCs where exactly the same thing happens.  And it’s not just that they split it up; it’s that on top of that, the content arrives half-baked. They keep pumping out Kits and Stuff Packs every other day, while the base game has bugs that have been there for years, completely ignored. The simulation feels empty at times, and instead of fixing the depth of the gameplay, they throw a new marketplace in our faces to keep scraping for microtransactions.  The final straw for many has been precisely this marketplace. In a game that already costs literally thousands of dollars to have in its entirety, adding an in-game store feels like a mockery.  It also depends way too much on mods. It’s unacceptable that, on top of how much the game already weighs with its DLCs, we have to download an extra 50 GB of mods just so the gameplay feels more natural. Honestly, if it weren't for the modding community working for free to fix bugs that have been there for years and to add the narrative depth that the development team seems incapable of creating, The Sims 4 would be an empty shell.  It doesn’t matter how much you enjoy decorating houses or creating characters; the monetization system is designed so that you never feel like you have the full game. It is a sales platform disguised as a life simulator. It’s a shame, because the artistic foundation and the Build Mode are fantastic, but EA’s management has turned The Sims 4 into a cynical product. I’d rather go back to installing The Sims 3, with all its performance issues, than continue feeding this money-grinding machine that respects neither the time nor the pockets of its community.

[b]Note:[/b] I have about 32 hours in Sims 4 outside of Steam.  I want to like Sims 4, and at its core, I do. There is care put into it, and at times it has some charm. They even made a lot of various improvements over the previous entries. All of it is just ruined by the insane greed with how highly priced most DLC is that should just be a part of the base game. Want to have pets? 40€ please. Want to have some basic weather? Another 40€ please. And even that DLC is platform locked, so if you want it both on Mac and Linux/Windows, you have to buy all DLC [i]twice[/i]. I've never seen a game platform lock DLC like that. I wouldn't even want to buy the entire game with all DLC for 40€, and now you want me to pay 160€ for DLC that should be part of the base game?? No, thanks.  It's also very buggy with EA not really showing any interest in fixing it. Some are minor bugs like grammatical errors (which are [i]very[/i] common with pronouns) and clipping issues, but there are also some major bugs, like game crashes and your sim just ignoring your commands for no reason. It's also very common for your sim to cancel their current action when you queue something, even if you didn't actually cancel it. Sometimes your sim can just cancel the action before even starting it, even if there's nothing else queued, which is very annoying.  The customisation is also way more limited than I would expect from a game where it should be a main feature. So many other games where you can customise your character, with it being far from the main feature, have better customisation. Want to change hair colour? Here are like 8 different colours to choose from. Want some piercings? Tough luck. It's so limited.  If the game wasn't so covered in greed, I could easily recommend it, but I doubt that will change at any point. I even bought the game when it still costed money, and what did I get for buying it? Nothing. Not even a "thanks".

Warning to new players and veterans: stop spending money on this game.    I have 450+ hours in *The Sims 4*. I bought 32 DLC packs. I regret it.    The full price of this game exceeds $1,600.  After all of that, the experience remains unstable, incomplete, and dependent on mods to function properly.    A new in-game marketplace with virtual currency has now been added on top of the existing DLC model.  Pricing structures are designed to encourage repeated spending through bundled currency and leftover balances.     Custom content creators receive roughly 30% while EA takes the majority.  The community that sustained this game is now another revenue channel.    Bugs persist. Packs release in poor condition. Core systems remain underdeveloped after more than a decade.    This model exists because players have supported it. I did. That support signalled that this level of pricing and monetisation was acceptable.    The result is a 12-year-old product built around extracting ongoing spending from a loyal player base.    If you are new, do not enter this ecosystem.  If you are a veteran, stop purchasing DLC and engaging with the marketplace.    The only meaningful response is to withdraw financial support.    Do not spend money on this.

[h1]The thousand dollar extortion simulator for ghost houses.[/h1]  Let us be honest right from the start. The base game is genuinely fun. The concept remains great. But let us be clear, visually, we are looking at an archaeological relic. The textures have the same freshness as a yogurt left in the sun for ten years. Fortunately, the formidable army of modders is here. These volunteer geniuses inject gigabytes of content so our characters stop looking like plastic potatoes. Sure, we do not reach the mind blowing realism of [b]inZOI[/b] which is already on the market and pulverizes this game on all levels, but we make do.  These independent creators do the work for free to keep this patient on life support. Meanwhile, what are the big bosses at Electronic Arts doing? They spawn broken expansions on an assembly line. I hold no grudge against the programmers doing what they can in the trenches, but there comes a time when you have to stop treating us like walking ATMs. To remain completely objective, I will only talk about the expansions I actually own and the bugs I have personally suffered. And believe me, the list is already long enough.  Let us take a little tour of the marvels they sell us at full price:  [list] [*][b]Get to Work:[/b] My customers wait eight hours at the cash register in a puddle of their own urine while aliens abduct my entire neighborhood. A beautiful day of hard work. [*][b]Get Together:[/b] My characters cancel absolutely every vital action to go dance the macarena in front of the stereo until total exhaustion. Impossible to make them eat. [*][b]Dine Out:[/b] An absolute marvel of simulation. I order a dish at noon, the waiter drops the plate three times, my character stares at the wall for four hours, and I finally get my cold meal at three in the morning. [*][b]Cats and Dogs:[/b] A wonderful simulator where I spend my entire life in the vet waiting room because my poor pet is sick every other morning and vomits rainbows. [*][b]Seasons:[/b] A masterclass. My characters decide to go out in their swimsuits in the middle of a blizzard to play in the snow and die of hypothermia with a massive radiant smile. [*][b]Discover University:[/b] My student starts walking to class three hours early but still misses the exam because they decided to admire a tree on the way or got stuck on an invisible bicycle. [*][b]For Rent:[/b] Let us save the best for last. The ultimate fantasy. I put on my contractor suit, I spend five hours of my life building and furnishing three apartments on the same lot. I place every little plant with love. I leave the lot to go to the world map. And when I come back... [i]Poof. Nothing left.[/i] The lot is entirely empty. It is exactly as if the local municipality sent a bulldozer during my absence to raze my buildings on the pretext that I did not have a building permit. Hours of my real life vaporized into the void because of a suffocating game engine. [/list]  Meanwhile, I can perfectly imagine the scene with the executives. They sit around a massive table, realize their latest game destroys our save files, and joyfully exclaim: [i]Perfect, let us announce a new furniture kit tomorrow morning![/i] They test absolutely nothing. All that matters is milking the cash cow to the very last drop.  To help us swallow this poisoned pill, we can count on the formidable courage of influencers. These youtubers who drop magnificent videos to explain why you absolutely must buy this new digital shipwreck. Of course they praise the product, they received it for free! They are terrified of losing their early access privileges. Their objectivity vanished at the exact same time as my houses.  And what about the official communication? Their presentation videos look like a bad joke. We see adults dressed in kindergarten clothes, talking to us with the forced enthusiasm of a children television host. They talk to veterans who have been playing for twenty years as if we were five year old kids incapable of understanding that we are getting beautifully scammed.  [b]In short:[/b] If you like spending a fortune on a building game where the city council comes to demolish your house the second you turn your back, go for it. Otherwise, keep your money, go play [b]inZOI[/b], and stop fattening a company that serves you spoiled mush with a big smile.

Rest in peace, Sims 4. This update killed what made the game special. Custom content and mods no longer work, and instead we’ve been given a new marketplace? What a disappointing replacement. Everything I've built in the sims over the years and years of playing is gone. All skin tones, makeup, clothes, hair, furniture, etc. gone. Now we have base game. Which is fine if you've never had CC but it (CC) has brought so much more life to the game and has ALWAYS been a huge part of the game for many PC players.  Why spend $5 for a content creator’s full catalog of custom furniture, clothing, makeup, etc. when you can now pay even more for a fraction of that in the new marketplace? EA has stripped away what made the community thrive and replaced it with SO MANY microtransactions. So, not only do we have expansion packs (which are fine and some do improve the game play), kits, collab content, we now have a marketplace to spend money on to enjoy the game? What once was a sandbox for imagination now feels like a storefront. This isn’t an improvement. This is a downgrade and a slap in the face to the player base in every way. (update: they noticed how upset the player base was and have since updated the game magically and CC now works, however, my review will remain :) )

Don't get me wrong - I love the Sims 4, but I simply wouldn't recommend anyone getting this. i am one of the fools who bought the Sims 4 and a DLC pack BEFORE they decided to make this free for all. Oh well, but no my rants aren't about that - it's about the constant forced updates. And with "updates" I don't mean "Bugfixes" but "random stuff you either can't use or don't want. Which means new creations from other users you don't care about or stuff for packs you don't own. Ignore this rant if you have a 1000 MBit line and play every day, but if you're that kind of occasional gamer who only runs this every few weeks and has a slow line - be prepared to wait an hour before the game actually launches. And if that ain't enough punishment - why were you here again? Oh yes, you use Steam. A pretty good ecosystem for gamers. And then comes The Sims 4 and adds another launcher - namely the EA launcher. Which will also waste your time with updates, additional account data that it occasionally forgets and lame ads. Seriously - if there'll ever be a new steam rule to ban all games that require an additional launcher - you got my vote. Nothing of value will be lost. Besides that? Sims 4 would probably be a great game. Yes they try to mooch you off with a hundred DLC packs, I really wanna know how many thousand bucks you would need to spend to own them all.
